heroOverview: Exertional heat stroke (EHS) is a life-threatening thermoregulatory emergency in which intense physical exercise overwhelms the body's ability to dissipate heat, driving core temperature above 40°C (104°F) and triggering central nervous system dysfunction — confusion, loss of coordination, or collapse. It is the leading cause of sudden death during sport and exercise in the United States, responsible for approximately 9,000 emergency department visits annually and an estimated 65-95 sport-related deaths per year. EHS is distinct from classic (non-exertional) heat stroke, which affects sedentary people during environmental heat waves; EHS strikes otherwise healthy young athletes and workers, often in hot but also in moderate environmental conditions. Prognosis is excellent when cooling begins within minutes — survival approaches 100% when core temperature is reduced below 39°C within 30 minutes. Delay in cooling is the single greatest determinant of death and organ damage, which is why the field management principle is: cool first, transport second.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 9,000 EHS-related emergency department visits annually in the United States (CDC); leading non-traumatic cause of sudden death in athletes
keyStats.demographics: Most common in male athletes aged 15-35; American football players in pre-season practice have the highest sport-specific incidence; military personnel in training are the second highest-risk group
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in young adults aged 15-30; a second group at elevated risk includes military recruits aged 18-25 in basic training
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global incidence is not reliably tracked; risk is highest in tropical and subtropical climates, in military training, and in endurance events (marathon, triathlon, cycling) globally

definition: Exertional heat stroke (ICD-10: T67.01 — Exertional heat stroke) is defined clinically by two criteria that must both be present: (1) a core body temperature above 40°C (104°F), measured rectally — axillary and oral measurements are unreliable — and (2) central nervous system dysfunction including confusion, disorientation, loss of coordination (ataxia), combativeness, loss of consciousness, or seizure. It is classified as exertional (type II heat stroke) to distinguish it from classic or non-exertional heat stroke (type I), which occurs without exercise in vulnerable populations during environmental heatwaves. The underlying pathophysiology involves a failure of thermoregulatory balance: exercise-generated heat production can reach 15-20 times the resting metabolic rate, and when environmental heat stress, high humidity, high clothing coverage, dehydration, or insufficient acclimatisation prevents adequate evaporative and convective heat loss, core temperature rises. The critical additional mechanism is a systemic inflammatory response triggered by endotoxin translocation from the gut (heat-induced gut permeability allows bacterial endotoxin to enter the circulation) combined with direct thermal cellular injury, causing multi-organ dysfunction including acute kidney injury, rhabdomyolysis, acute liver failure, disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), and cardiac arrhythmia. EHS falls within sports medicine and emergency medicine; immediate on-site treatment is as important as hospital management.

typesClassification.description: Occurs in healthy young individuals during intense physical activity; heat production overwhelms the body's cooling capacity even without extreme ambient heat. Sweating is usually still present at the time of collapse, distinguishing it clinically from classic heat stroke.

Occurs in the elderly, the very young, or people with chronic illness during environmental heatwaves without strenuous activity; sweating may be absent. Managed similarly but EHS progresses faster and is more often associated with rhabdomyolysis.

EHS accompanied by one or more organ failures — acute kidney injury, rhabdomyolysis with CK above 50,000 U/L, coagulopathy (DIC), cardiac injury (troponin elevation), acute liver failure, or status epilepticus. Requires ICU management and carries higher mortality.

primarySymptoms: Core body temperature above 40°C (104°F) measured rectally — the cardinal objective finding; this threshold must be sought actively because oral and axillary temperatures consistently underestimate core temperature during exercise.

Confusion, disorientation, or altered mental status — the athlete cannot give their name, state the date, or follow simple commands; this is the most important clinical indicator of heat stroke rather than heat exhaustion.
Ataxia and loss of coordination — the athlete staggers, cannot walk a straight line, or falls; a quick field test is asking the athlete to walk heel-to-toe in a straight line.
Combativeness, agitation, or uncharacteristic bizarre behaviour during or just after exercise in a hot environment.
Collapse or loss of consciousness — ranges from a brief syncope to prolonged coma in severe cases.
Seizures — generalised tonic-clonic seizures occurring in up to 15-30% of EHS cases during or after collapse, reflecting profound brain temperature elevation.
Hot, flushed skin that is usually wet (sweating present in most EHS cases, unlike classic heat stroke).
Rapid heart rate (tachycardia) and rapid breathing (tachypnoea) as the cardiovascular system attempts to redirect blood to the skin for cooling.
Nausea and vomiting preceding or accompanying collapse, often the first symptom reported by the athlete before confusion develops.
Cessation of sweating in late, severe EHS when dehydration has exhausted fluid stores — an ominous sign indicating failure of the primary evaporative cooling mechanism.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden slowing or stumbling in an athlete who was previously performing normally, without an obvious mechanical cause.
An athlete appearing confused, unresponsive to questions, or 'not themselves' during intense exercise in heat.
Reported severe throbbing headache and intense dizziness that does not resolve with brief rest.
Muscle cramps, nausea, and feelings of extreme weakness in hot conditions preceding the onset of neurological symptoms.
emergencySigns: Any confusion, disorientation, combativeness, or loss of consciousness during or after exercise — this is heat stroke until proven otherwise and requires immediate cold-water immersion.
Rectal temperature above 40°C — confirm with a rectal thermometer; do not wait for oral or tympanic temperature.
Seizure during or after vigorous exercise in heat.
Cardiac arrest or absent pulse in a collapsed athlete after exercise — begin CPR immediately; cooling should proceed in parallel.
Persistent unconsciousness more than 30 seconds after exercise cessation in a hot environment.

causes.cause: Exercise-induced heat production exceeding dissipation capacity
causes.description: Intense exercise can generate 15-20 times the resting metabolic heat rate. When sweat evaporation — the dominant cooling mechanism — is impaired by high humidity, tight or impermeable clothing, or excessive heat production from a sprint or prolonged effort, core temperature rises at rates of 1°C per 3-5 minutes, reaching 40°C within 15-30 minutes of inadequate cooling.

High ambient temperature reduces the temperature gradient driving convective heat loss, while high relative humidity (above 75%) impairs sweat evaporation, the most effective cooling pathway. The wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT) combines these parameters; exercise restrictions are recommended when WBGT exceeds 28°C for unacclimatised individuals.

A body water deficit of even 2% reduces plasma volume, skin blood flow, and sweat rate, impairing thermoregulation. By 3-4% dehydration, core temperature rises 0.2-0.3°C higher for the same exercise intensity versus euhydrated state. EHS risk is substantially elevated in athletes who are hypohydrated before exercise begins.

The body adapts to heat stress over 7-14 days of heat exposure, increasing plasma volume, improving sweat rate and onset, and reducing cardiovascular strain for a given heat load. Athletes or military recruits beginning exercise in heat without prior acclimatisation — especially in the first 3-5 days — are at substantially higher risk; approximately 70% of military EHS cases occur in the first week of training.

Multiple agents increase EHS risk by reducing sweating (anticholinergics, antihistamines, antipsychotics), increasing heat production (stimulants — amphetamines, MDMA, cocaine), reducing thirst (diuretics), or increasing metabolic rate (thyroid supplements, beta-agonist misuse). A thorough medication review is part of every EHS investigation.

causes.cause: Individual susceptibility and prior heat illness
causes.description: A history of previous heat stroke markedly increases risk of recurrence, suggesting persistent impairment of thermoregulatory capacity in some individuals. Sickle cell trait, certain rare mitochondrial myopathies, and anhidrosis (inability to sweat) are specific medical risk factors.

riskFactors.factor: Exercising in hot and humid conditions without acclimatisation
riskFactors.description: The combination of high WBGT and unacclimatised status is the most powerful modifiable risk factor for EHS; pre-season American football camps and military basic training in summer produce the highest numbers of cases precisely because unfit, unacclimatised individuals perform maximal effort in peak summer heat.

Beginning exercise in a hypohydrated state or failing to replace sweat losses during prolonged exercise reduces plasma volume and impairs evaporative cooling; urine colour monitoring and pre-exercise weighing are simple, validated prevention strategies.

riskFactors.factor: Excessive or impermeable clothing and protective equipment
riskFactors.description: Full American football equipment, military battle dress, or chemical protection suits can reduce sweat evaporation by 50-80%, dramatically raising the core temperature for a given workload; equipment design and scheduled rest periods are critical risk mitigation tools.

Amphetamines, MDMA (ecstasy), cocaine, and ephedrine raise metabolic heat production and impair thermoregulation; they are implicated in multiple exertional heat deaths in military and sports settings.

Sickle cell trait (SCT) increases EHS risk, particularly under conditions of intense sustained exercise and dehydration; the US military found a 30-fold increased risk of exertional heat death in SCT carriers compared with non-carriers during basic training. Modified training protocols now mitigate this risk.

Young men dominate EHS statistics, reflecting their higher proportion in high-risk activities (contact sport, military training), higher sweat rates that can accelerate dehydration, and potentially higher motivation to ignore early warning signs.

riskFactors.factor: Febrile illness or gastroenteritis within the preceding 48 hours
riskFactors.description: Fever raises baseline core temperature; concurrent fluid losses from illness plus exercise dehydration create a compounding risk. Returning to training within 48 hours of a febrile illness dramatically increases EHS susceptibility.

A history of EHS is associated with persistent thermoregulatory impairment in a significant minority of survivors; return to sport after EHS requires careful laboratory heat tolerance testing before clearance.

affectedDemographics: Male athletes aged 15-35 in endurance and collision sports, especially during pre-season training
Military recruits and soldiers in basic training, particularly in the first week
Marathon, triathlon, and long-distance running participants in warm-weather races
Outdoor manual workers — construction, agriculture, landscaping — in summer
People with sickle cell trait performing intense sustained exercise
Individuals who are deconditioned or unacclimatised beginning an intense exercise programme in summer
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of exertional heat stroke is clinical and must be made in the field — delaying cooling while awaiting formal hospital diagnosis is the primary preventable cause of EHS mortality. The diagnosis requires two criteria: a rectal temperature above 40°C and central nervous system dysfunction. No other temperature measurement (oral, axillary, tympanic, forehead) is accurate enough to replace rectal temperature in a collapsed athlete. Any athlete who collapses during or immediately after exercise in heat and shows confusion, ataxia, seizure, or loss of consciousness must be treated as EHS until proven otherwise. In the hospital, the diagnostic workup quantifies multi-organ involvement and guides supportive care. Complete blood count reveals haemoconcentration and thrombocytopenia (DIC). Comprehensive metabolic panel shows elevated creatinine (AKI), elevated liver enzymes (AST, ALT often in the thousands within 24-48 hours), elevated bilirubin, and electrolyte abnormalities — hypo- or hypernatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia. Creatine kinase (CK) quantifies rhabdomyolysis: values above 5,000 U/L are consistent with significant muscle breakdown; above 50,000 U/L indicates severe rhabdomyolysis requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation. Coagulation studies (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer) detect DIC. Urine myoglobinuria — urine that is dark brown or cola-coloured — confirms rhabdomyolysis. Troponin elevation reflects myocardial stress injury. Brain CT or MRI is obtained if altered consciousness persists after normothermia is achieved to exclude concurrent traumatic brain injury or cerebral oedema.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The only accurate, readily available method for establishing core temperature in a collapsed athlete; identifies EHS when temperature exceeds 40°C and guides the endpoint of cooling (below 39°C).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lubricated rectal thermometer is inserted 10 cm into the rectum; the reading is available within 30-60 seconds. This measurement is taken in the field or emergency room, not in the scanner or during transport.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies skeletal muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis); serial CK measurements guide the aggressiveness of intravenous fluid therapy and predict risk of acute kidney injury.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample from a vein in the arm; results are typically available within 1-2 hours in an emergency laboratory. CK peaks 24-48 hours after the injury and is tracked over several days.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive metabolic panel and liver function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses kidney function (creatinine, BUN), electrolytes, blood glucose, and liver enzymes (AST, ALT) to detect acute kidney injury and heat-induced liver damage, both of which develop within hours of EHS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 hours. Liver enzymes may be normal on the first blood test and rise sharply over 12-24 hours, requiring repeat testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Coagulation screen (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, platelet count)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), a serious coagulopathy triggered by heat-induced endothelial injury; DIC significantly increases bleeding risk and requires targeted treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw in a citrate tube sent to the laboratory; results available within 1-2 hours. In DIC, PT and aPTT are prolonged, fibrinogen is low, D-dimer is markedly elevated, and platelets are falling.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects dark urine from myoglobin released by damaged muscle cells; myoglobinuria confirms rhabdomyolysis and predicts acute kidney injury risk — myoglobin is directly nephrotoxic at high concentrations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urine sample in a cup; dipstick shows positive for blood without red cells on microscopy (classic myoglobinuria pattern). Confirmatory myoglobin assay is sent to the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for cardiac arrhythmias and ST-T changes from direct thermal myocardial injury or electrolyte disturbance; serum potassium must be checked immediately as severe hyperkalaemia from rhabdomyolysis can cause fatal arrhythmia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard 12-lead ECG with electrodes attached to the chest, arms, and legs; results available within minutes. Continuous cardiac monitoring is maintained throughout the acute hospital phase.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of exertional heat stroke treatment is rapid whole-body cooling, initiated at the point of collapse, before transport. Every minute above 40°C increases the probability of permanent organ damage. The principle of 'cool first, transport second' is endorsed by all major sports medicine organisations including the NATA, ACSM, and British military guidelines. Cold-water immersion (CWI) in ice water at 0-15°C is the most effective cooling method, reducing core temperature at 0.2-0.3°C per minute and achieving the target below 39°C in 6-15 minutes for most patients; it is the gold-standard when available. Field-feasible alternatives include cold wet towels with ice packs to the neck, axillae, and groin combined with fans (less effective, rate 0.04-0.05°C/min), cold intravenous fluids, and cold water dousing. Evaporative cooling with wet sheets and electric fans offers intermediate efficacy. Hospital treatment continues cooling until rectal temperature reaches 39°C, at which point cooling is stopped to prevent overshoot hypothermia. Supportive ICU care addresses the multi-organ consequences: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline, target urine output 200-300 ml/hour) to prevent myoglobin-induced AKI; renal replacement therapy (dialysis) if AKI is severe; fresh frozen plasma and cryoprecipitate for DIC; anticonvulsants for seizures; and liver support measures for severe hepatic injury. Antipyretics (paracetamol, NSAIDs) do not reduce core temperature in EHS because the thermostat set-point is normal — the problem is excess heat production, not an elevated set-point — and are not used for cooling. Dantrolene is not effective in EHS (unlike malignant hyperthermia) and is not recommended. Discharge from ICU follows stabilisation of organ function, typically over 2-7 days; return to exercise is permitted only after formal laboratory heat tolerance testing in specialised facilities.

medicalTreatments.name: Cold-water immersion (CWI)
medicalTreatments.description: Immersion of the body (excluding head) in an inflatable cooling tub or bath filled with water at 0-15°C, ideally in the field at the site of collapse. Agitation of the water or rotation of ice improves heat exchange. Cooling is continued with rectal temperature monitoring until core temperature reaches 39°C.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cooling rate of 0.2-0.3°C per minute; achieves 39°C target within 6-15 minutes for most athletes; near-100% survival when CWI is applied within 30 minutes of collapse in published military and sports series.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation (normal saline or Hartmann's solution)
medicalTreatments.description: Large-volume IV fluids, typically 3-10 litres in the first 24 hours, maintain kidney perfusion, promote myoglobin clearance through forced diuresis, and correct dehydration. Target urine output of 200-300 ml/hour prevents myoglobin precipitation in renal tubules.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation reduces rates of acute kidney injury requiring dialysis in EHS patients with rhabdomyolysis; a key component of standard ICU protocols in all major guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzodiazepines for seizure control
medicalTreatments.description: IV lorazepam (0.1 mg/kg) or diazepam is first-line for active seizures; persistent seizures may require levetiracetam or phenytoin. Cooling is the definitive seizure treatment in EHS since seizures are driven by brain temperature elevation, but anticonvulsants are needed while cooling is established.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Benzodiazepines terminate initial seizures in over 80% of cases; seizures typically do not recur once core temperature is below 39°C.

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (dialysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous veno-venous haemofiltration or intermittent haemodialysis for severe acute kidney injury with oliguria unresponsive to fluid resuscitation, or for hyperkalaemia threatening arrhythmia from massive rhabdomyolysis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Required in approximately 5-30% of EHS cases with severe rhabdomyolysis (CK above 50,000 U/L); most patients who require dialysis recover kidney function within 4-8 weeks if the acute phase is survived.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood product support for DIC
medicalTreatments.description: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP), cryoprecipitate (for fibrinogen replacement), and platelet transfusions are given for clinically significant coagulopathy with active bleeding. Treatment of DIC in EHS is primarily via cooling, which removes the thermal stimulus; blood products provide bridging support.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive transfusion reduces bleeding complications in EHS-associated DIC; DIC typically resolves within 24-48 hours after successful cooling in survivors.

alternativeTreatments.name: Evaporative cooling with misting fans
alternativeTreatments.description: Spraying water onto exposed skin combined with forced air circulation from electric fans; useful when ice water immersion is not available. Less effective than CWI (cooling rate approximately 0.04-0.05°C/min) but widely used in race medical tents as a practical field alternative.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ice packs to high-flow vascular regions
alternativeTreatments.description: Applying ice packs or cold wet towels to the neck, axillae, and groin — regions where large blood vessels run close to the skin — accelerates cooling when CWI equipment is unavailable. Always used as an adjunct, not as a substitute for CWI when CWI is feasible.

whySeeSpecialist: Any athlete who has experienced EHS should be evaluated by a sports medicine physician before returning to exercise. Laboratory heat tolerance testing — a standardised graded exercise test in a controlled hot environment — is the only way to determine whether thermoregulatory capacity has fully recovered after EHS. Approximately 10-20% of EHS survivors have persistent heat intolerance on formal testing, and clearance to return to sport or military duty depends on passing these tests. Specialist evaluation also identifies underlying conditions (sickle cell trait, rare sweat gland disorders) and provides structured return-to-sport progression.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute EHS management, emergency medicine and critical care physicians are the immediate providers. For return-to-sport evaluation, a sports medicine physician with access to a dedicated heat tolerance testing laboratory is important; such facilities exist at major university sports medicine programmes and military medical centres. Ask whether the clinic uses a validated heat tolerance test protocol and has experience managing post-EHS athletes.
preventionStrategies: Acclimatise gradually: begin exercise in heat at reduced intensity, progressing over 7-14 days to allow plasma volume expansion and improved sweat response before full training load.
Monitor and control wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT): follow event and training modification guidelines — reduce intensity or cancel when WBGT exceeds 28°C for unacclimatised individuals.
Ensure adequate pre-exercise hydration: urine should be pale yellow; begin exercise euhydrated and replace fluids during exercise at a rate matching sweat losses.

Have a cold-water immersion tub and rectal thermometer at every high-risk event and practice: the ability to begin CWI within minutes of collapse is the most impactful preventive measure against EHS death.
Educate coaches, athletes, and trainers to recognise early CNS signs and to treat confusion or ataxia during exercise as heat stroke until proven otherwise.
Modify or exclude athletes from heat exercise within 48 hours of febrile illness, gastroenteritis, or significant dehydration.
Review and adjust medications with anticholinergic, diuretic, or stimulant properties for athletes exercising in heat.
Screen athletes with sickle cell trait and adapt training accordingly: avoid maximal sustained efforts in the heat without adequate rest and hydration.
lifestyleModifications: Never attempt to train through confusion, severe headache, or loss of coordination in hot weather — these require immediate cooling.
Wear light-coloured, loose, moisture-wicking clothing during exercise in heat; avoid dark, tight-fitting, or impermeable fabrics.
Schedule the most intense training for cooler parts of the day — early morning or evening — during summer months.
Log daily body weight before and after exercise to quantify fluid losses; each 1 kg of weight lost equals approximately 1 litre of sweat deficit.
Never use stimulant recreational drugs before exercise, especially in hot conditions.
After recovering from EHS, complete a formal heat tolerance test before returning to sport — do not self-assess readiness.

recommended: Water and electrolyte drinks (sodium content 400-800 mg/L) to replace both fluid and sodium lost in sweat during prolonged exercise
Regular meals and snacks before exercise to avoid hypoglycaemia, which compounds thermoregulatory impairment
Cool or cold pre-exercise meals and drinks, which lower starting core temperature by 0.3-0.5°C and delay onset of heat strain
Foods rich in potassium and magnesium — bananas, potatoes, leafy greens — to support electrolyte balance during heavy sweating
Adequate carbohydrate intake, as glycogen depletion impairs heat tolerance through increased metabolic heat production at low work rates
avoid: Alcohol in the 24 hours before exercise in heat: alcohol causes diuresis, raises baseline core temperature, and impairs hypothalamic thermoregulation
Caffeine at high doses before prolonged exercise in severe heat, as it modestly raises metabolic heat production, though moderate amounts are generally safe for acclimatised individuals
Plain water without electrolytes as the sole replacement fluid during exercise exceeding 2 hours, which can cause exercise-associated hyponatraemia (dangerously low blood sodium)
Weight-cutting practices (sweat suits, diuretics, restricting fluids) common in combat sports — these dramatically increase EHS risk

exerciseGuidelines: During EHS recovery, exercise must stop completely until medical clearance. The return-to-sport (RTS) progression after EHS is gradual and formally supervised. Most sports medicine programmes use a stepwise protocol: Phase 1 — light activity in cool conditions at 2-4 weeks after resolution of all organ dysfunction; Phase 2 — progressive aerobic training at 4-6 weeks with heat monitoring; Phase 3 — full supervised training in controlled heat with temperature monitoring; Phase 4 — formal laboratory heat tolerance test at 8-12 weeks, which is required before unrestricted return. Approximately 80-90% of athletes pass heat tolerance testing by 3 months and return to full sport. Athletes who do not pass require further evaluation and individualised return timelines.
dailyManagement: During exercise in heat: monitor for any confusion, stumbling, or unusual behaviour in yourself or teammates — act immediately and do not attribute it to fatigue.
Check urine colour before exercise: aim for pale yellow; dark yellow or amber means drink more fluid before starting.
Know your nearest cold-water immersion facility: if you train or compete in heat, ensure an ice bath or inflatable cooling tub is available within metres of the exercise area.
After EHS recovery: attend all follow-up appointments and do not rush back to sport; the risk of recurrent EHS after premature return is high.
Medical alert: carry a card or wear a medical alert bracelet noting a history of heat stroke if you are returning to exercise, to inform any emergency responder.
prognosis: The prognosis of exertional heat stroke is almost entirely determined by the speed of cooling. When core temperature is reduced below 39°C within 30 minutes of collapse — achievable with immediate cold-water immersion at the event site — survival approaches 100% and the incidence of permanent organ damage is very low. Delay in cooling beyond 30-45 minutes substantially increases multi-organ failure rates: acute kidney injury requiring dialysis occurs in 15-30% of delayed cases, acute liver failure in 5-10%, and neurological injury (cerebellar dysfunction, memory impairment, personality change) in 5-20%. Mortality in delayed EHS reaching hospital without pre-hospital cooling is reported at 5-30% in military series. Even with good initial survival, approximately 10-20% of EHS survivors demonstrate persistent heat intolerance on laboratory testing, limiting return to prior levels of sport or military duty. Most athletes who complete a supervised return-to-sport protocol and pass heat tolerance testing return to full sporting activity within 3-6 months.
recoveryTimeline: In the hospital, organ function typically begins to stabilise within 24-48 hours after successful cooling; liver enzyme elevations peak at 24-72 hours and normalise in most cases over 1-4 weeks. Rhabdomyolysis-associated kidney injury resolves in 1-4 weeks in most survivors; dialysis-requiring AKI may persist for 4-8 weeks before recovery. Return to light activity begins at 2-4 weeks for uncomplicated cases. Formal heat tolerance testing is performed at 8-12 weeks. Full return to competitive sport with clearance typically occurs at 3-6 months for athletes who pass heat tolerance testing.
complications: Acute kidney injury — occurs in up to 30-50% of EHS cases with severe rhabdomyolysis; myoglobin clogs and damages renal tubules; most resolve with aggressive fluid therapy, but dialysis is required in severe cases.

Rhabdomyolysis — massive release of myoglobin, potassium, and other intracellular contents from heat-damaged skeletal muscle; CK may reach hundreds of thousands of units per litre; causes AKI and dangerous hyperkalaemia.

Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — heat-induced endothelial injury and cytokine storm trigger systemic coagulation activation, consuming clotting factors and platelets; produces simultaneous thrombosis and haemorrhage.
Acute liver failure — heat-induced hepatocellular necrosis causing coagulopathy, jaundice, and encephalopathy; AST and ALT may exceed 10,000 U/L in severe cases and require liver support in an ICU.

Neurological injury — cerebellar dysfunction, memory impairment, and personality change from direct thermal brain injury; the cerebellum is particularly heat-sensitive; persists in a minority after apparent recovery.

Cardiac arrhythmia and injury — direct thermal myocardial damage (troponin elevation in approximately 50% of EHS cases) and electrolyte disturbances from rhabdomyolysis can cause ventricular arrhythmias.

supportResources.name: NATA Position Statement — Exertional Heat Illness in Athletes
supportResources.description: Comprehensive professional guidance from the National Athletic Trainers' Association on prevention, recognition, and management of EHS and other exertional heat illnesses.

supportResources.name: CDC — Heat Stress and Related Illness
supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance on occupational and sports-related heat illness, including prevention strategies and first-aid protocols.

supportResources.name: American College of Sports Medicine — Heat and Hydration
supportResources.description: Evidence-based recommendations from the ACSM on exercise in the heat, hydration, and heat illness prevention for athletes and coaches.

sources.title: Casa DJ et al. National Athletic Trainers' Association Position Statement: Exertional Heat Illnesses. Journal of Athletic Training, 2015
Bouchama A, Knochel JP. Heat Stroke. New England Journal of Medicine, 2002
McDermott BP et al. National Athletic Trainers' Association Position Statement: Fluid Replacement for the Physically Active. Journal of Athletic Training, 2017
Epstein Y, Yanovich R. Heatstroke. New England Journal of Medicine, 2019
Lugo-Amador NM et al. Heat-related illness. Emergency Medicine Clinics of North America, 2004
Gauer R, Meyers BK. Heat-related Illnesses. American Family Physician, 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epileptic seizures occur independently of exercise and heat; a seizure during exercise in heat must be assumed to be EHS until a rectal temperature is measured. Prior epilepsy history alone does not exclude EHS in a collapsed hot athlete.

confusedWithConditions.name: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DIC is a complication of EHS, not a separate mimic; however, the bleeding manifestations of EHS-associated DIC can be mistakenly attributed to other coagulopathies unless the exercise and heat context is recognised.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AKI from rhabdomyolysis is a complication of EHS, not a mimic; however, in a patient presenting with myoglobinuria and renal failure in the absence of recorded core temperature, the heat stroke trigger may be overlooked if the history of exercise in heat is not sought.

relatedConditions.name: Sickle cell disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Sickle cell disease and trait both increase exertional heat stroke risk; sickling in muscle microvessels during intense exercise in heat creates a compounding mechanism for rhabdomyolysis and multi-organ failure.

relatedConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
relatedConditions.relevance: AKI from myoglobin-induced tubular damage is the most common serious complication of EHS with rhabdomyolysis, occurring in 15-50% of hospitalised cases and requiring dialysis in severe instances.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes impairs autonomic thermoregulation, sweat gland function, and cardiovascular response to heat; diabetic patients performing exercise in heat require extra attention to hydration and early recognition of heat illness.

relatedConditions.name: Hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Antihypertensive medications — particularly thiazide diuretics, beta-blockers reducing cardiac output, and alpha-blockers causing vasodilation — can impair thermoregulation and increase EHS risk during exercise in heat.

faqs.question: What is the difference between heat exhaustion and heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Heat exhaustion causes heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, and dizziness but the person remains alert and orientated. Heat stroke means the person is confused, disoriented, combative, or unconscious — a key distinction. Any confusion during exercise in heat means heat stroke, not exhaustion, and requires immediate cooling in ice water rather than rest and rehydration.

faqs.question: How do you treat exertional heat stroke in the field?
faqs.answer: Immerse the body in ice water (0-15°C) immediately — this is the most effective cooling method available and should be started before calling for an ambulance. Remove excess clothing, monitor breathing, and check rectal temperature every 5 minutes. Stop cooling when rectal temperature reaches 39°C. Do not give fluids by mouth to a confused person.

faqs.question: Can heat stroke cause permanent damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. When core temperature remains above 40°C for more than 30 minutes, permanent damage to the brain (especially the cerebellum), kidneys, and liver can occur. Approximately 10-20% of survivors have lasting impairments. Immediate cooling within minutes of collapse prevents almost all permanent organ damage.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for exertional heat stroke?
faqs.answer: When cold-water immersion cooling begins within 30 minutes of collapse, survival approaches 100% in published athletic trainer and military series. Delayed cooling beyond 30-45 minutes sharply increases mortality; overall reported mortality in hospitalised cases ranges from 5-30% depending on the delay to cooling.

faqs.question: How hot is too hot to exercise outside?
faqs.answer: The wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT) integrates heat, humidity, and radiation. Most sports medicine guidelines recommend reducing intensity when WBGT exceeds 28°C for unacclimatised individuals, and cancelling high-intensity activity when it exceeds 32°C. In practice, any combination of temperature above 32°C and humidity above 60% represents high risk for those exercising intensely.

faqs.question: Why do you use rectal temperature and not oral or ear temperature?
faqs.answer: Oral and tympanic (ear) temperatures consistently underestimate core temperature by 0.5-2°C in athletes during and after exercise, often missing the critical threshold above 40°C that defines heat stroke. Only rectal temperature accurately reflects core temperature at the site of organ injury; this is why sports medicine guidelines require rectal measurement for diagnosing and managing heat stroke.

faqs.question: Can you get heat stroke in mild weather?
faqs.answer: Yes. Exertional heat stroke occurs through internal heat production from intense exercise, not just from external heat. In highly fit athletes performing maximal efforts, or in individuals wearing impermeable clothing and equipment, core temperature can reach dangerous levels even at ambient temperatures as low as 21-24°C if heat dissipation is impaired.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated EHS with prompt cooling typically resolves within 1-2 weeks for the acute phase. Return to exercise follows a supervised protocol over 8-12 weeks, ending with a formal heat tolerance test. Most athletes who pass return to full activity by 3-6 months. Those with organ complications may take 2-6 months longer.

faqs.question: Does sickle cell trait increase heat stroke risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sickle cell trait (not the same as sickle cell disease) is associated with a 30-fold higher risk of exertional heat death in military studies. The combination of intense exercise, dehydration, and heat promotes sickling in muscle microvessels, accelerating rhabdomyolysis and collapse. Modified training protocols and awareness dramatically reduce this risk.

faqs.question: Can antipyretics like paracetamol reduce heat stroke temperature?
faqs.answer: No. Paracetamol and NSAIDs lower fever by resetting the hypothalamic temperature set-point, but in heat stroke the set-point is normal — the problem is overwhelming heat production. Antipyretics have no cooling effect in heat stroke and should not be used for this purpose. Physical cooling with cold water or ice is the only effective treatment.

faqs.question: What should you eat and drink before exercise in the heat?
faqs.answer: Drink 400-600 ml of water or an electrolyte drink 2 hours before exercise, and check urine is pale yellow. Eat a regular meal for energy; avoid alcohol the previous evening. During exercise exceeding one hour, drink 400-800 ml of electrolyte fluid per hour rather than plain water alone, which can cause low blood sodium if consumed in large amounts.

faqs.question: Can heat stroke happen to fit, young athletes?
faqs.answer: Yes — exertional heat stroke predominantly affects young, fit athletes, not the elderly. Physical fitness does not protect against EHS when the exercise intensity and environmental heat load exceed the body's cooling capacity. High motivation, competitive drive, and absence of a pre-existing illness actually increase EHS risk by encouraging athletes to push through warning signs.

faqs.question: Why is cold water immersion the best treatment?
faqs.answer: Cold water immersion (CWI) cools the body 4-6 times faster than ice packs to the axillae and neck, reducing core temperature at 0.2-0.3°C per minute. It conducts heat away from the entire body surface simultaneously. Studies comparing CWI with other methods consistently show faster time to safe temperature and better survival outcomes.

faqs.question: Can medications cause heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anticholinergic drugs (certain antihistamines, bladder medications, antipsychotics) impair sweating; diuretics cause dehydration; stimulants (amphetamines, MDMA, cocaine) dramatically raise heat production; beta-blockers reduce cardiac output needed for skin blood flow. Any athlete on these medications should inform their medical team before training in heat.

faqs.question: Can someone return to playing sport after heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes, for approximately 80-90% of athletes, but only after formal medical clearance. A structured return-to-sport protocol spanning 8-12 weeks is followed, ending with a standardised laboratory heat tolerance test. Athletes who pass return to full sport; those who fail undergo further evaluation and may have temporary or permanent activity restrictions.

faqs.question: What happens to the kidneys in heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis) releases myoglobin into the blood; myoglobin precipitates in the renal tubules and is directly toxic to kidney cells, causing acute tubular necrosis. This produces the characteristic dark brown (cola-coloured) urine. Aggressive IV fluid therapy flushes myoglobin through the kidneys; without it, acute kidney injury requiring dialysis develops in up to 30-50% of severe EHS cases.

faqs.question: Does acclimatisation prevent heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Acclimatisation substantially reduces EHS risk but does not eliminate it. Over 7-14 days of progressive heat exposure, the body adapts with a 10-15% increase in plasma volume, earlier and heavier sweating, and a lower heart rate for the same workload. Approximately 70% of EHS cases in military training occur in the first week before meaningful acclimatisation occurs.

faqs.question: How is exertional heat stroke different from classic heat stroke?
faqs.answer: Exertional heat stroke affects healthy young people during intense physical activity; sweating is usually still present. Classic heat stroke affects the elderly and medically vulnerable during heat waves without exercise; sweating is often absent. Both require the same emergency cooling treatment, but EHS progresses faster and is more commonly associated with rhabdomyolysis and multi-organ failure.

faqs.question: What role does humidity play in heat stroke risk?
faqs.answer: High humidity impairs sweat evaporation — the body's primary cooling mechanism. When humidity exceeds 75-80%, sweat cannot evaporate effectively even at lower air temperatures, trapping heat in the body. At 32°C with 90% humidity, the effective heat stress is similar to 40°C dry heat. This is why WBGT, which accounts for humidity, is used to set exercise restrictions rather than air temperature alone.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise alone in hot weather?
faqs.answer: Exercising alone in hot conditions significantly increases EHS risk because confusion and collapse may go unnoticed. Buddy systems, regular check-ins, and training in areas with immediate access to medical support are recommended. The most preventable EHS deaths occur when athletes collapse alone and cooling is delayed because no one is nearby to recognise the emergency.

metaTitle: Exertional Heat Stroke: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Exertional heat stroke is a life-threatening exercise emergency. Learn symptoms, emergency cooling, organ complications, and prevention strategies.
